pattern: A one-dimensional dynamic program: the cheapest way to stand on a step is its own cost plus the cheaper of the two steps you could have arrived from, built bottom-up.
hints: Think about the minimum cost to be standing on each step, not the cost to reach the very top directly.
To stand on step i you must have paid cost[i] and come from either step i-1 or step i-2, so take the cheaper arrival.
Carry two running minimums for the previous two steps; the answer is the cheaper of the final two, since the top sits just past the last step.

edge_inputs.description: three-step staircase from the first example
longer staircase where skipping the expensive steps wins
minimum length, pick the cheaper single step
all zeros, cost should be zero
alternating cheap and expensive favours skipping

stress.description: random staircases of varied length within value bounds
stress.note: exercises the linear recurrence across many lengths
